card.title: How Fingerprints Caught Criminals
card.teaser: Every fingertip is a one-in-a-billion signature. How did we turn a smudge on a glass into courtroom evidence?
card.coverImagePrompt: A close-up of a detective's gloved hand dusting a dark glass tumbler with fine aluminum powder, a ghostly fingerprint emerging under warm desk lamp light, cinematic noir, shallow focus, vintage evidence bag blurred in background

lessons.title: The smudge that hanged a killer
lessons.body: In 1905, two elderly shopkeepers in Deptford, London, were found beaten to death. The only clue was a greasy thumbprint on an empty cash box. It was the first time in British history a fingerprint alone would send someone to the gallows — and it launched a revolution in how crimes are solved.

visual.imagePrompt: A dented metal cash box on a Victorian shop counter, a single oily thumbprint visible on its lid, dim gaslight atmosphere

lessons.title: Why no two prints match
lessons.body: Fingerprints form in the womb between weeks 10 and 24, shaped by random pressure on fetal skin as the baby grows. Even identical twins — who share DNA — have different ridge patterns. The three basic shapes are loops, whorls, and arches, but it's the tiny break points called minutiae that make each print unique.

keyTerms.definition: The tiny ridge endings and bifurcations that make each print individually identifiable
The skin layer beneath the fingertip that carries the permanent ridge pattern

visual.imagePrompt: A macro photograph of a fingerprint with minutiae highlighted by glowing red dots, scientific overlay style

lessons.title: From Argentina to Scotland Yard
lessons.body: The first recorded fingerprint conviction happened in 1892 in Argentina, when police inspector Juan Vucetich matched a bloody thumbprint on a doorpost to a mother who had murdered her own two children. Scotland Yard adopted the Henry Classification System in 1901, sorting prints into ten categories so detectives could actually search their files — no small feat in a pre-computer world.

visual.imagePrompt: A 1900s-era Scotland Yard archive room, wooden drawers of paper fingerprint cards stretching into shadow, single hanging lamp

lessons.title: How latent prints are lifted today
lessons.body: Most prints at a crime scene are 'latent' — invisible until developed. Investigators use fine powders on hard surfaces, cyanoacrylate (superglue) fuming on porous ones, and ninhydrin on paper, which turns amino acids from sweat a bright purple. Modern AFIS databases then compare an unknown print against tens of millions of stored records in seconds.
lessons.funFact: Koalas have fingerprints so similar to humans' that they've reportedly confused forensic investigators at Australian crime scenes.

visual.imagePrompt: A purple-stained paper document under UV light revealing vivid fingerprints, laboratory setting with gloved hands

lessons.title: Not quite as perfect as TV suggests
lessons.body: Fingerprint matching isn't actually a hard science — it relies on a trained examiner's judgment. Studies since the 2000s have shown error rates of around 0.1%, and several wrongful convictions have been traced back to mistaken matches. The ridges on your fingers may be unique, but the humans reading them are not infallible.

quiz.question: When do fingerprint patterns form?
options: At birth, from contact with air
Between weeks 10 and 24 of fetal development
During the first year of life
quiz.explanation: Ridge patterns are set in the womb by random pressure on developing fetal skin, which is why even identical twins have different prints.

quiz.question: What are 'minutiae' in fingerprint analysis?
options: The three main pattern types (loop, whorl, arch)
Powders used to reveal latent prints
Small ridge endings and forks that individualize a print
The sweat residues left behind on surfaces
quiz.explanation: Minutiae are the small break points and bifurcations in ridges that examiners compare to declare a match.

quiz.question: Which substance reveals fingerprints on paper by reacting with amino acids?
quiz.explanation: Ninhydrin turns amino acids from sweat a vivid purple, making it ideal for porous surfaces like paper.

sources.title: Suspect Identities: A History of Fingerprinting and Criminal Identification
Accuracy and reliability of forensic latent fingerprint decisions
sources.publisher: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
